description: A14FJ - Day 4, part 6: Descent Orbit Insertion

tokens: A successful SPS burn has turned Apollo 14 into a manned satellite of the Moon.
Al Shepard, Stu Roosa and Ed Mitchell have filled the radio waves with their appropriately awed reactions to viewing the Moon up close while speeding around it.
Another burn will soon be performed to adjust their orbit into one that will facilitate the lunar landing the upcoming day.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 84 hours, 21 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've had Loss of Signal as Apollo 14 coasted around behind the Moon on this first lunar revolution.
We presently showing orbit measuring 58.5 nautical miles [108.3 km] pericynthion by 168.8 nautical miles [312.6 km] apocynthion.
And within about 15 minutes the change of shift briefing with Gold Team Flight Director Gerry Griffin, in the Houston News Center.
Meanwhile, the Orange team with Pete Frank in charge is taken over in the control center here.
At 84 hours 22 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: For the time they are beyond communications range, the DSE records their systems data on the tape, for review once they are back over the near side of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The DSE tape recorder is usually operated remotely from Mission Control using what are called real-time commands.
Radio signals can be sent onboard to operate only two pieces of equipment - the DSE and the Master Alarm, which can be remotely triggered to rouse the crew.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Command Module telecommunications system is highly complex, and requires them to operate and verify no less than 26 switches to select the functions they require for the moment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Fluids expelled from the spacecraft can create false stars that impede the crew's ability to identify real stars through their optics.
The expulsions also generate a small amount of thrust, which can affect their trajectory.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verb 82 retreives the parameters of their orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In a spacecraft the size of the Apollo Command Module, there is no room for privacy, and henceforth, modesty.
This is evident from Al and Ed's frank conversation about Ed's plans.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Davy is named after the British chemist Sir Humphry Davy (1778-1829), best known as the discoverer of the elements potassium, sodium and calcium.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 84 hours 59 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 14.
We're less than 5 minutes away now from predicted time of acquisition of the spacecraft now in lunar orbit.
The lunar orbit insertion burn was literally right on the money.
Our predicted versus actual numbers read as follows: predicted Ground Elapsed Time for the maneuver was 82 hours 36 minutes 42.7 seconds.
The actual time for the maneuver, 82 hours 36 minutes 42.7 seconds.
Predicted delta V, 3022.7 feet per second [921.3 m/s], actual delta V, 3022.4 feet per second [921.2 m/s].
The duration of the burn predicted 6 minutes 12.28 seconds.
We're 3 minutes 30 seconds away from time of acquisition and we'll stand by and continue to monitor.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Two minutes away now from time of acquisition.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Farouk El-Baz (1938 - ), the Egyptian-born geology expert at the core of the Apollo geological science program.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Langemak is named after Russian rocket scientist Georgij Langemak 1898-1938).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I've got a few PADs for you when you get a chance to copy.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordon, if it's the landmark tracking pad, I'm ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Before you do, if you give usP00and Accept, we'll give you a new state vector, and I have the Mösting A pad, ready to go.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: T1is 85:39:35; T2, 85:46:39;TCA, 85:51:39; and T3is 85:54:09; north, 8 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Your readback is correct; and next page, I have map update Rev 3.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The Map Update PAD is interpreted as follows:REV:3rd orbit around the MoonLOS:086:29:12 - Loss of Signal180°:086:53:05 - the time the CSM crosses the 180th meridian on the far side of the Moon, signifying the start of a new orbitAOS:087:15:06 - Acquisition of Signal
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's correct, and, about three pages on, the H-3 landmark pad if ready.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: T horizon is 87:49:48;TCA, minus 20 seconds at 87:51:47.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We got stopped about halfway through the update to theTEI-4 pad.
If you or Ed is ready, I can finish that one off.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: A new Noun 81, plus 3907.1, plus 0703.8, minus 0081.8; and change the roll to 182.
Pitch and yaw remain the same, and everything I didn't read is the same as the first version.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: But first of all, the weight was 38230; and that Tig91:16:06.90; Noun 81, plus 3907.1, plus 0703.8, minus 0081.8; and roll, 182; pitch, 069; yaw, 012.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That's all the updates I have at the moment, though we're still standing by on theTEI-5 pad.
You may keep that page ready when - we'll have it shortly.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And we're through with the uplink.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're standing by - your null bias reports.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It will be about 2 minutes, Gordon.
I had to restart it in.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Apollo 14 now showing at an orbit of 168.6 nautical by 58.8 nautical miles [108.9 km].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, minus 100 at the start; minus 99.4.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: At 85 hours 16 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.

Conversation that has transpired thus far on the second revolution has been with - principally with Command Module Pilot Stu Roosa and Lunar Module Pilot Ed Mitchell and we have not heard from the commander Alan Shepard thus far this pass.
We're at 85 hours 17 minutes into the flight and this is Apollo Control Houston, continuing to monitor.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At 85 hours 24 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Here's some preliminary numbers for theDOIor descent orbit insertion pad, which we do expect will be passed to the crew sometime during the front side pass.
The burn is presently scheduled to occur at Ground Elapsed Time of 86 hours 50 minutes 54.97 seconds, with a burn time of 20.77 seconds, and aDelta-Vof 206.4 feet per second [62.9 m/s].
At 85 hours 25 minutes Ground Elapsed Time continuing to monitor, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have theDOIandTEI-5 pads, when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'll start with theDOI.SPS/G&N; 38155; plus 1.45, minus 0.71; TIGis 086:50:54.97; Noun 81, minus 0206.4, minus all balls, minus 0003.6; attitude is 000, 281, 000; Noun 44, 0058.8, plus 0009.1; 0206.4; burn time, 020.8, that's 20.8;Delta-VC0199.6; 11, 199.0, 33.9; all the rest of the items on the pad are N/A.
Comments:GDCaligns at the stars, Sirius on the zero degree mark and Rigel.
Other comments: TheDelta-Vcounter should read minus 6.7 at cut-off.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Sirius on the zero degree line and Rigel with 127, 148, 015; Ullage is four jets, 14.
And, other remarks,Delta-Vcounter - it should read minus 6.7 at cut-off.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This PAD is for a burn to place them onto an orbit with very low perigee on the near side of the Moon for the purpose of the LM descent.System:The burn would be made using the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):38,155 pounds (17,307 kg).
Note that since the LM weight hasn't really changed much (nothing much has been consumed), a previously used value is assumed.Pitch and yaw trim angles (Noun 48):+1.45° and -0.71°.

These angles represent an initial direction for the gimbal-mounted engine in order to fire through the predicted centre of mass of the combined spacecraft.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):86 hours, 50 minutes, 54.97 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, -206.4 (-62.9); Y, 0 (0); Z, -3.6 (-1.1).
These velocity components are measured with respect to the local vertical.
The large negative value of the X component shows that the burn is retrograde, i.e.
It will slow them down and have the effect of lowering the altitude of their orbit at a point on the other side of the Moon from where the burn takes place.
A zero value for the Y component shows that there is no change to the orbit's inclination.Spacecraft attitude at TIG:Roll, 0°; Pitch, 281°; Yaw, 0°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.

These attitude angles are with respect to the orientation of the guidance platform.HA, expected apocynthion of resulting orbit:58.8 nautical miles, or 108.9 km.HP, expected pericynthion of resulting orbit:9.1 nautical miles, or 16.9 km.Delta-VT:206.4 fps (62.9 m/s).
This is the total velocity change experienced by the spacecraft and is a vector sum of the three components given above.Burn duration or burn time:20.8 seconds.Delta-VC:199.6 fps.

This value is entered into the EMS to allow it to have backup control of the engine.Sextant star:Star 11 (Aldebaran, Alpha Tauri) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 199.0° and 33.9° respectively.

This is part of an attitude check.GDC (Gyro Display Coupler) Align stars:Sirius and Rigel to be used for GDC Align in case the IMU is unavailable for attitude reference.GDC Align angles:Roll, 127°; pitch, 148°; yaw, 015°.The additional notes point out that when carrying out a GDC Align using the stars, it is Sirius that gets placed on the zero-degree mark in the telescope's reticle.
Unusually, the code numbers for Sirius and Rigel are mentioned.
SPS propellants are settled in their tanks by firing the plus-X thrusters on all four quads around the Service Module for 1.4 seconds.
Finally, as the burn progresses and the Delta-V counter on the EMS counts down, it is expected that it will read -6.7 fps at cut-off.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Under comments: Number 1, burn is undocked, and number 2, assumedDOI.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And before you start your readback, Ed, a reminder to Stu; about 30 seconds now to T1for Mösting A.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay.TEI-5 PAD:SPS/G&N; 36697; minus 0.63, plus 0.21; 092:30:20.01; plus 3023.5, plus 1091.5, minus 0171.2; 182, 107, 021; four jet, 11 seconds.
Under remarks: Number l, it's an undocked burn; number 2, it assumesDOI.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: An interpretation of the PAD follows:Purpose:The burn would be carried out at the end of Rev-5 in case the mission had to be aborted.System:It would use the large SPS (Service Propulsion System) engine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):36,697 pounds (16,645 kg).
Note that, compared to the DOI PAD read up a few minutes ago, this value is smaller to take account of the estimated propellant used during DOI.Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.63° and +0.21°.

These angles represent an initial direction for the gimbal-mounted engine in order to fire through the predicted centre of mass of the spacecraft.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):92 hours, 30 minutes, 20.01 seconds.
As with all the TEI PADs, the burn is timed for when the spacecraft is over the far-side.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,023.5 (+921.6); Y, +1,091.5 (+332.7); Z, -171.2 (-52.2).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the local vertical.
The large positive X component shows that this burn would be prograde, i.e.
in the same direction as their orbital motion, increasing their velocity so that they escape the gravitational pull of the Moon.
The Y component indicates that the plane of their orbit would be changed to one which intersects Earth.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 182°; Pitch, 107°; Yaw, 21°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.Other items on the standard form are not applicable (NA) to this PAD.
There are the following additional notes.
SPS propellants would be settled in their tanks by firing the plus-X thrusters on all four quads around the Service Module for 11 seconds.
The burn data assumes that the CSM is no longer carrying the weight of the LM and that they would be aborting the mission from the descent orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Ed, I have one more comment for you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We noticed on the last burn when we played back the data, that you started the recorder about 12 minutes prior to the burn.
For this one, we'd like you to wait until about 1 minute prior to the - to the burn to start the recorder so that we can get a quicker playback on the data for verification of the burn afterAOS.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we'll give you this one real late.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The next burn is theDOIwhich will reduce the altitude of the front side pass from 312 km down to a mere 17 km above the surface.
Since this burn is done behind the Moon,FIDOand his backroom specialists will have to wait until the spacecraft reappears before they can look at the burn data and obtain new tracking data.
They will then have only 12 minutes to make sure that the burn went as planned and that the spacecraft will not descend too low.
Just in case, as soon as they finish theDOIburn, they will set the spacecraft up for a bailout burn.
If it appears that they would be descending too low, they could then do the bailout burn to immediately stop their descent.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You heard CapCom Gordon Fullerton passing along theDOIandTEIon five PADs.
TheTEI5 PAD is for contingency purposes only and it's stored within the spacecraft.
We presently show Apollo 14 in an orbit of 168.4 nautical miles [311.9 km] by 58.7 nautical miles [108.7 km].
Assuming the Descent Orbit Insertion burn would give us a resultant orbit of 58.8 nautical miles [108.9 km] by 9.1 nautical miles [16.9 km].
We're at 85 hours 42 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Not a great deal of conversation with the crew of Apollo 14 on this the second revolution.
Reading the onboardDSKYshows the crew of Apollo 14 now performing a program 24.
This is a optic tracking landmark tracking activity.
Off their register they are now showing their shaft and trunnion angles.
We'll stand by and continue to monitor in the event further conversation sues.
We show a bit over 1 hour, 5 minutes from scheduled time of ignition for the Descent Orbit Insertion burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And you have about 10 seconds to T2.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We have a beautiful sight on the crater Eratosthenes, Timarcus [sic], Wallace.
Timarcus [sic], I mean Timocharis, is just as - just on my horizon as we approach the terminator.
Eratosthenes is about halfway to the horizon.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Crater Eratosthenes is named after the Ancient Greek astronomer and geographer, famous for calculating the circumference of the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Timocharis is named after the Ancient Greek astronomer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater Wallace is named after Alfred Russel Wallace, the British biologist and naturalist, a pioneer of evolution theory.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're standing by - the magazine percentage.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The magazine now reads 77; and, just for info, I lost the landmark about 20 seconds earlier than what it shows.
But we got a good track on it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We're reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Hey, Gordon, how do you read 14?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Is 060 still the roll attitude you want us on this Verb 49 maneuver?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 86 hours Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 14 now on it's second revolution around the Moon.
For the descent orbit insertion burn the onboard computer program 40 will be used as this is the service propulsion system thrust program.
We expect to see 14 - the crew of 14 start into that program sequence and maneuver to burn attitude before they pass out of range on this revolution.
The burn, of course, will be done at 86 hours 50 minutes 54 seconds when the spacecraft is out of contact with Mission Control.

At time of ignition minus 30 seconds the onboard computer starts counting down to the burn at TIGminus 5 seconds the proceed button must be pushed probably by spacecraft commander Al Shepard for the countdown to continue.
Otherwise, the maneuver will not be accomplished.
We're at 86 hours 2 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We show our Mission Control clock at - we're 48 minutes 48 seconds from time of ignition.
And this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have you on the high gain.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I have you on the high gain, locked up.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I hear you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Our displays showing the computer readouts from the spacecraft show that Apollo 14 is now in the process of aligning the guidance and navigation platform.
We're at 86 hours 9 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
And, Apollo 14 presently in a orbit of 168.4 nautical miles [311.9 km] apolune 58.7 nautical miles [108.7 km] perilune.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Did you get the torquing angle?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This is Stu's 11th P52 realignment of the guidance platform during the flight.
As a reference, he sighted on star 16 (Procyon) and star 17 (Regor).
As a check of his sighting accuracy, the computer compared the measured angle between these stars and the actual angle.
The difference between them was 000.01 degrees.
Finally, the computer displayed Noun 93, the angles by which the gimbals would be rotated or 'torqued' to restore perfect alignment.
These were -0.050° in X, -0.070° in Y and -0.045° in Z axis.
According to the post-flight Mission Report, the platform was torqued at 86:10 GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, if you'll give usP00and Accept, we'll give you a new state vector and a target load.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The computer is yours, and we're standing by to watch P30 and P40.
We noticed on the high gain that the signal strength's a little different.
Did you change any configuration on the comm or - and - and did you change position of your squelch on the last high-gain acquisition?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're at standard lunar comm and I don't know if we've done anything differently.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We can recheck everything, but it'll take a minute.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: All the comm switches are where they were set on the backside.
Nothing's been changed except acquiring with the high gain.
I did switch the - the Squelch Enable when we were on Omni and I was looking for you - for a good Omni during the maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Our display show the onboard computer for the display keyboard just switching from program 30 this is the externalDelta-Vprogram, prethrusting program.
to program 40 which is the service propulsion system, engine program.
This is a program in which the crew of Apollo 14 will perform the descent orbit insertion burn.
We've got a little over 11 minutes before we loose signal with Apollo 14 as it passes over the backside of the Moon.
86 hours 18 minutes into the flight, this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Mission Control Flight Director Pete Frank now taking a status from members of his flight control team for a Go/No-Go forDOI.
Presently coming up all greens on his console.
Fullerton will pass along a Go momentarily.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Spacecraft to Commander Alan Shepard responding to that call.
We're at 86 hours 20 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
And, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston, 86 hours 26 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We show a little over 3 minutes from time of loss of signal.
As Apollo 14 now close to passing out of range on this the second revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, read you loud and clear, Ed.
And about 2 minutes toLOS; a minute and 45, now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Less than 1 minute now from Loss of Signal.
Our clock in Mission Control shows time of ignition now 22 minutes 30 seconds away.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've had Loss of Signal with Apollo 14 as it - now passing over the back side of the Moon.
On this pass the descent orbit insertion burn will be performed near perilune.
The command service moduleDOIburn is performed with the service propulsion system engine and this with the spacecraft at an essentially retrograde attitude and the crew heads down.
Following theDOIburn the vehicle is maneuvered to the required attitude for a possible preplanned contingency burn called the bail out burn.
Initial burn attitude is such that the vehicle is pitched up some 73 degrees above the local horizontal.
At the bail out burn time - the bail out burn time, this is at acquisition of signal plus 12 minutes.
Assuming the likelihood that the bail out burn will not be required, the command and service module is maneuvered to an attitude which allows the visual observation of landmark, H3, with the optics.
As you would expect, the bail out burn is one case where the mission control centers fondest hope is to get no go for the maneuver.
We're at 86 hours 31 minutes into the flight and this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control Houston, 86 hours 50 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Our ignition clock in mission control shows 50 seconds away from scheduled time of ignition for the descent orbit insertion burn.
This burn scheduled with a Ground Elapsed Time of 86 hours 50 minutes 55 seconds.
And if successful, placing Apollo 14 in an orbit approximately 58.8 nautical miles [108.9 km] by 9.1 nautical miles [16.9 km].
10 seconds, 5, we should be burning at this time if going according to plan.
The ground elapsed clock now reads 86 hours 51 minutes into the flight of Apollo 14.

In the mission control center the ignition clock will shortly be switched to a secondAOSclock giving - with these two clocks, the top one assuming a no burn early acquisition of signal, the bottom one an on time burn.
The clocks have now been switched.
86 hours 52 minutes Ground Elapsed Time this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Crater Dellinger is named after the American physicist John Howard Dellinger (1886-1962).
Crater Pannekoek is named after Antonie Pannekoek (1873-1960), the Dutch astronomer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 87 hours 12 minutes into the flight of Apollo 14.
Our countdown clocks in Mission Control, the no burn clock 45 seconds away now, continuing to count.
On our own time burn clock we show 3 minutes 9 seconds away.
We'll stand by and watch as we draw closer to the time of acquisition.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've passed the early time and continuing to count.
Our own time countdown clock shows 2 minutes 18 seconds away from acquisition.
Meanwhile, in Mission Control, a larger than usual turnout for this hour.
In the Viewing room is Acting Administrator of NASA, George Low, Manned Spacecraft Center Director, Dr.

Gilruth, and on the control room floor the backup crew of Apollo 14, backup commander Gene Cernan, Ron Evans, backup Command Module Pilot, Joe Engle backup Lunar Module Pilot, Rocco Petrone, director of the Apollo Program, Chris Craft, deputy director of the Manned Spacecraft Center, Sig Sjoberg, director of flight operations, Astronaut Chief Tom Stafford, and director of flight operations, Deke Slayton.
Just a little over a minute away now from scheduled time of acquisition.
This is Apollo Control, Houston, standing by.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Also in the viewing room, Dale Myers, associate administrator of NASA from Manned Space Flight and Dr.
John Clark director of Goddard Space Flight Center.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Standing by, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: A lot of people standing by to hear how it went.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Burn time was 20.6 seconds with the following residuals on theG&Nshutdown: plus 0.6, plus 0.2, zero;Delta-VC, minus 6.2.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Copied burn time, 20.6; a plus 0.6, plus 0.2, and a zero; andDelta-VC, minus 6.2.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like you to go toP00, please.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll have a look at the VG.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Onboard orbit reading reported by Al Shepard 10.4 nautical miles [19.3 km] by 58.4 nautical miles [108.2 km].
Earlier reported by spacecraft commander Shepard a burn time of 20.6 seconds.
We're at 87 hours 18 minutes Ground Elapsed Time .
transcript.speaker: PAO
